lesson: c1-l04 / How Large Language Models Work
title: How Large Language Models Work

scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: How large language models actually work. [pause] You don't need to be an engineer — but understanding the mechanism transforms how you use these tools.

scenes.name: Why it matters
scenes.text: When you understand the mechanism, three things change. [pause] You stop being surprised by hallucinations. You write better prompts. And you make better decisions about when to trust the output.

scenes.name: What is an LLM
scenes.text: A large language model is <emphasis>not</emphasis> a database. [pause] Not a search engine. Not a system that understands meaning. [pause] It's a <emphasis>pattern machine</emphasis> — trained on billions of pages of text to learn what kinds of words follow other words.

scenes.name: Tokens
scenes.text: LLMs don't read whole words. They work with <emphasis>tokens</emphasis> — chunks of text. The word „understanding" might be split into „under" and „standing." [pause] This is why AI sometimes can't count letters in a word — it doesn't see letters, it sees chunks.

scenes.name: Process
scenes.text: The generation process. [pause] You type a prompt. The model breaks it into tokens. It predicts the most likely next token. Adds it. Predicts the next. [pause] Word by word. One token at a time.

scenes.name: No planning
scenes.text: Important detail. [pause] The model does <emphasis>not</emphasis> plan its response in advance. [pause] It builds it word by word, each one predicted from everything before it. [pause] That's why it can write a beautiful explanation — and make a factual error in the next sentence.

scenes.name: Context window
scenes.text: Context is the model's <emphasis>working memory</emphasis>. [pause] It sees your whole conversation, not just the last message. Modern models hold hundreds of pages. [pause] When conversation gets too long, the earliest parts drop out. The AI doesn't „forget" — it literally lost the data.

scenes.name: Reads like
scenes.text: Why LLMs seem to think. [pause] They've learned what well-reasoned text <emphasis>looks like</emphasis>. [pause] They reproduce the pattern of logical argument without performing logical reasoning. [pause] Think of it as the world's most well-read parrot.

scenes.name: For practice
scenes.text: What this means for your practice. [pause] Write clearer prompts — better patterns mean better results. Verify factual claims — dates, statistics, citations. [pause] Provide context all at once — don't rely on memory across many messages.
